chunks.text:
Modern life is shaped by systems.

Transportation networks. Financial infrastructure. Software platforms. Energy grids. Organizational processes. Communication networks.

We rarely think about these systems directly. Instead, we interact with their surfaces: the interfaces, tools, and services that expose them to us.

When those surfaces are well designed, the system feels intuitive. When they are poorly designed, the system feels confusing or hostile.

But the real problem is often deeper than the interface.

Many systems are simply too complex for the humans operating them.

This complexity does not appear overnight. It accumulates gradually as technologies evolve, organizations scale, and new layers of functionality are added. Over time, the system becomes harder to understand, harder to operate, and harder to trust.

The result is friction everywhere.

People create workarounds. Organizations create new roles to manage complexity. Entire industries emerge to coordinate systems that were never designed to fit together in the first place.

At that point, the challenge is no longer one of interface design. It becomes a challenge of system design.
